title: Paperclip Sucks, Actually
summary: Nick Saraev argues that Paperclip is overhyped and encourages looking for better alternatives. He positions himself as someone with practical real-world automation experience, having built automated businesses generating seven-figure revenue over six years.
writeup: A sharp polemic against agentic framework theater, centering on Paperclip and similar multi-agent orchestration tools that generate elaborate internal workflows without producing any external deliverable. The creator dissects real social-media posts where people describe running AI CEOs, COOs, and task-delegation hierarchies, then asks what any of it actually shipped. The through-line is a consistent demand for economic reality: tools are means, not ends, and the question is always what got done and how much money was made.

key_points: Public posts showcasing Paperclip setups, including a 'zero human company' terminal demo and a 30-day TikTok experiment, show agents doing internal research, platform evaluation, and dashboard UX improvements, none of which are external-facing deliverables a client or customer ever sees.

Copying a human organizational hierarchy (CEO delegating to CMO, CTO, COO) onto AI agents is arbitrary; AI systems are massively parallelizable and excel at tightly scoped tasks, so the right structure would spin up 50 parallel agents on the same deliverable, calculate the mode and median of outputs, and collapse them, rather than mimicking a 100-year-old management ladder.

The Apple Notes meme captures the core error: the most effective operators use simple tools and high agency, while median performers accumulate Readwise, Anki, Notion, and orchestration wrappers that substitute tool complexity for actual output.
Social media and YouTube financially incentivize creators to amplify every new framework as a breakthrough, which structurally detaches the hype from whether anything economically useful was built.
The creator's own benchmark for evaluating any tool or workflow is a single question, attributed to Elon Musk's reply to Parag Agrawal: 'What did you get done this week?'
